coredata.dc:title: A novel “off-on-off” fluorescent-nanoprobe based on B, N co-doped carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets for sensitive detection of GSH and Ag+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jun
Yang, Huan
Pan, Shuang
Liu, Hui
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new “off-on-off” fluorescence strategy for detecting glutathione (GSH) and silver ions (Ag+) is presented. The constructed nanoprobe is composed of B, N co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) and manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 nanosheets), where MnO2 nanosheets serve as a kind of efficient fluorescence quencher. The sensing mechanism of the system is based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and destruction-protection strategy. The assay strategy includes three processes: fluorescence quenching of B, N-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets, the deconstruction of MnO2 nanosheets by GSH, the combination between GSH and Ag+. The MnO2 nanosheets are reduced to Mn2+ because of the addition of GSH and restoring the fluorescence intensity of the system, while the formation of the complex between GSH and Ag+ inhibit the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets on account of the addition of Ag+, leading to the decrease in fluorescence of the probe. This strategy allows the quantitative detection of GSH and Ag+ with detection limit of 0.32 μmol·L−1 and 0.24 μmol·L−1, respectively. Moreover, this approach displays good sensitivity, selectivity and broad linear range, which could be broadly applicable for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B
N-CDs
MnO2 nanosheets
Fluorimetric assay
GSH and Ag+

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric detection of GSH and Ag+
Fluorescence detection of GSH and Ag+
Detection mechanism of GSH and Ag+
Selectivity for determination of GSH and Ag+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of B, N-CDs. (B) the size distribution histogram of B, N-CDs. (C) XRD spectrum of B, N-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of B, N-CDs.
(A) The XPS spectrum of B, N-CDs. High resolution spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C) and B 1s (D), respectively.
(A) TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets. (B) FT-IR spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets. (C) The wide scan XPS spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets. (D) High resolution spectra of Mn 2p.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of B,N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (290 to 380 nm). (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue line), emission spectra (green line) and excitation spectra (red line) of B, N-CDs.
(A) Effects of ion strength on fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs. (B) pH stability of B,N-CDs. V CDs = 200 μL, λex = 320 nm.

The effect of pH on the detection of GSH (A) and Ag+ (B). The effect of reaction time on the detection of GSH (C) and Ag+ (D). (E) The effect of the volume of MnO2 nanosheets. V CDs = 200 μL, c GSH = 80 mol·L−1, c Ag+ = 40 mol·L−1, λex = 320 nm, pH 7.0.

Detection of GSH. (A) The emission spectra of the B, N-CDs/MnO2 system with the addition of various concentrations of GSH (0 to 90.0 μmol·L−1). (B) Relationship between F − F
0 and the concentration of GSH. F and F
0 refer to the FL intensity with GSH and without GSH. V CDs = 200 μL, λex = 320 nm, pH 7.0. Detection of Ag+. (C) The emission spectra of the B, N-CDs/MnO2/GSH system in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+ (0 to 70.0 μmol·L−1). (D) Relationship between F
0 − F and the concentration of Ag+. F and F
0 refer to the FL intensity with Ag+ and without Ag+. V CDs = 200 μL, λex = 320 nm, pH 7.0.

(A) The FL intensity of B, N-CDs, B, N-CDs + MnO2 nanosheets, B, N-CDs + GSH, B, N-CDs + Ag+, B, N-CDs + GSH + Ag+, B, N-CDs + MnO2 nanosheets +GSH, B, N-CDs + MnO2 nanosheets + GSH + Ag+. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets and the excitation and emission spectrum of B, N-CDs. (C) Fluorescence decays of B, N-CDs in the absence and presence of MnO2 nanosheets. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of B, N-CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and B, N-CDs + MnO2 nanosheets.

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system in the presence of diverse interfering substances (80 μmol·L−1 for GSH, 80 μmol·L−1 for AA and Cys, 800 μmol·L−1 for other interferents). (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs/MnO2/GSH system with the addition of diverse metal cations (40 μmol·L−1 for Ag+; 400 μmol·L−1 for other metal cations, and EDTA was added into the solution of Cu2+ as mask regent).
Schematic illustration of the fluorometric sensing for GSH and Ag+ based on B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for GSH and Ag+ detection.
Results of the determination of GSH and Ag+ in real samples. (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A novel “off-on-off” fluorescent-nanoprobe based on B, N co-doped carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets for sensitive detection of GSH and Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new “off-on-off” fluorescence strategy for detecting glutathione (GSH) and silver ions (Ag+) is presented. The constructed nanoprobe is composed of B, N co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) and manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 nanosheets), where MnO2 nanosheets serve as a kind of efficient fluorescence quencher. The sensing mechanism of the system is based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and destruction-protection strategy. The assay strategy includes three processes: fluorescence quenching of B, N-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets, the deconstruction of MnO2 nanosheets by GSH, the combination between GSH and Ag+. The MnO2 nanosheets are reduced to Mn2+ because of the addition of GSH and restoring the fluorescence intensity of the system, while the formation of the complex between GSH and Ag+ inhibit the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets on account of the addition of Ag+, leading to the decrease in fluorescence of the probe. This strategy allows the quantitative detection of GSH and Ag+ with detection limit of 0.32 μmol·L−1 and 0.24 μmol·L−1, respectively. Moreover, this approach displays good sensitivity, selectivity and broad linear range, which could be broadly applicable for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver, known as a kind of noble metal, has attracted considerable attention in the respect of electrical materials [1], photographic materials [2], catalysts [3] and jewelry [4] for its stable physical and chemical properties. However, excessive silver ions (Ag+) may cause harmful influence on the human health and environment [5]. On the other hand, glutathione (GSH, L-γ-glutamyl-L-cysteinyl-glycine) refers to the most ample thiolated tri-peptide in biological systems [6,7]. The biological activity of GSH originates from the sulfhydryl group (-SH), which serves as a proton donor [8]. Glutathione plays a critical part in sustaining redox intracellular, and guarding against toxins and radicals in cellular [9,10]. The increase in the level of GSH may cause cancers [11], cardiovascular disease [10], cystic fibrosis [12] and so on. Consequently, there is an urgent need to accurate detection of GSH and silver ions with an uncomplicated and efficient method. On this ground, a number of methods have been established for GSH detection and silver ions detection，such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering [12], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], electrochemical methods [4,14], fluorimetric methods [1,15] and atomic spectrometry [16]. Among all those methods, fluorometry exhibit significant superiorities, such as simple, effective, low-cost and nondestructive [17].

Carbon dots (CDs), have attracted tremendous attention owing to its non-toxicity, high photostability, biological compatibility and environmental-friendly capacity [18,19]. To obtain high emission efficiency carbon dots, the heteroatomic doping (such as B, N, P, S) approach is a convenient pathway to be explored for enhancing the fluorescent properties, which could passivate the surface active sites and enhance quantum yield (QY) [20]. Furthermore, multiple heteroatoms doping have drawn broad attention on account of their synergistic effect of the doped heteroatoms in carbon dots [21]. For example, Nanda et al. synthesized blue-emissive boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanoparticles with a QY of about 14.2% for determination of picric acid [22]. Kanwal et al. have reported the bright fluorescent N, B co-doped carbon nanodots (B, N-CDs) by using the boric acid and N-(4-hydroxyphenyl) glycine as the source of boron and nitrogen [23].

MnO2 nanosheets are one of the most popular 2D nanomaterials over the years. The large surface area, broad absorption spectrum, and ultrahigh drug-loading capacity offer numerous opportunities for MnO2 nanosheets to be used in various fields of optoelectronics, nanomedicine and biosensors [24,25]. The broad absorption peak located at 380 nm endows MnO2 nanosheets with high fluorescence quenching efficiency [26]. In addition, the MnO2 nanosheets could be reduced by glutathione (GSH), leading to the destruction of MnO2 nanosheets. Thereafter, Qu's group designed a fluorescence “turn off-on” assay for sensing of glutathione by using CDs–MnO2 nanocomposites [27]. However, the linear range of this method was relatively narrow, which limited its application in real time analysis.

Inspired by the previous works, we designed a convenient fluorescence “off-on-off” platform by employing B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system for the detection of GSH and Ag+ (Scheme 1 ). In the proposed system, MnO2 nanosheets acted as an efficient fluorescence quencher which could quench the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs. However, reductive GSH could reduce Mn (IV) of the MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, accompanying the recovery of the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs. Moreover, when Ag+ was added, the negatively charged sulfhydryl group of the GSH had a strong affinity with Ag+ [28], which put an end to the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets. MnO2 nanosheets not only served as fluorescence quencher and recognition units for GSH, but also improved the sensitivity of the detection of GSH. Changes in the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs were recorded, which made them a signal reflecting the variation of the concentration of GSH and Ag+. The constructed probe provided a simple, sensitive and environmental friendly method for monitoring GSH and Ag+.

All the chemicals were directly used without any further purification. Boric acid and MnSO4 were obtained from Chongqing Beibei Chemical Reagent Company (Chongqing, China). Ethylenediamine was bought from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was supplied by Sanland Chemical Co, Ltd. (Los Angeles, USA). L-glutathione reduced (GSH, 98.0%) and AgNO3 were from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The B, N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were characterized by using XRD (AXS-D8, Bruker, Germany), TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA), XPS (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi), FTIR (FTIR-8400S, Kyoto, Japan), UV–vis spectra (UV-2700 Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) and FL spectra (F-2500 Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan).

B, N-CDs was fabricated by hydrothermal method using boric acid and ethylenediamine as the source of boron and nitrogen. In brief, 0.3 g boric acid and 200.0 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water, then heating at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the B, N-CDs was obtained by purifying for 12 h to remove small molecules for future use.

The synthesis of MnO2 nanosheets was completed by a previously reported method with minor modification [29]. Typically, 3.4 mg MnSO4 and 1.0 mg BSA were mixed in a beaker with 10.0 mL water and kept stirring for 1 h. Then, 1.0 mol·L−1 NaOH solution was introduced to the above solution to adjust the pH about 10.0. The obtained MnO2 nanosheets were centrifuged and washed with water and ethanol for three times after stirring for overnight. Finally, the dark brown precipitate was dried at 60 °C and dispersed in 10.0 mL ultrapure water for future use.

The quantum yield of the B, N-CDs was determined in reference to quinine sulfate (QY of 54% when excited at 360 nm). The formula of QY measurement is as follows:
Φ u
=
Φ s
F u
F s
A s
A u
where s and u refer to the reference quinine sulfate and B, N-CDs solution, respectively. Φ represents the quantum yield, F means fluorescence peak area (λex = 360 nm) and A is the absorbance intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of GSH and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: For GSH detection, 200.0 μL of different concentrations of GSH solution and 700.0 μL of the Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH 7.0) was added into 400.0 μL MnO2 nanosheets solution and incubated for 20 min, then 200.0 μL B, N-CDs solution was added. Finally, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at 320 nm excitation wavelength.

For Ag+ detection, a solution of 200.0 μL GSH solution (0.6 mmol·L−1) was mixed with 200.0 μL different concentrations of Ag+. Thereafter, BR buffer solution, 400.0 μL MnO2 nanosheets and 200.0 μL B, N-CDs solution were added into the mixture, and the final volume of the reaction solution was 1.5 mL. At last, the fluorescent spectrum of the mixture was obtained with the proposed method.

The TEM image (Fig. 1A) of as prepared B, N-CDs showed a well separated size distribution, which it could be observed that B, N-CDs are nearly monodispersed and spherical. Fig. 1B showed the average particle size was 3.94 nm and the particle size distribution of B, N-CDs which ranged from 2.75 to 5.75 nm. In Fig. 1C, the XRD pattern of the B, N-CDs appeared a broad diffraction peak at 23°, which ascribed to the amorphous structure of synthesized B, N-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of B, N-CDs was shown in Fig. 1D. The peaks located at 3405 cm−1 and 1643 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO, respectively [30]. The peak at 1575 cm−1 was due to the bending vibration of NH bonds [31]. The absorption peaks at 1431 cm−1, 1334 cm−1, and 1057 cm−1 corresponded to the BO stretching vibration [32], CN stretching vibration [18], and CB stretching vibration [31], respectively.

Fig. 2A showed the XPS survey of B, N-CDs. The XPS spectrum of B, N-CDs presented the chemical composition of B, N-CDs, which were primarily composed of B, C, N and O. And the four peaks at 190.8, 284.9, 399.8 and 532.9 eV attributed to the B 1s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, severally. In Fig. 2B, the three peaks at 284.6, 285.4 and 286.4 eV in the C1s spectrum belonged to the C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and CO bonds, severally [33]. The N 1s high-resolution spectrum showed two obvious peaks at 399.5 and 400.6 eV corresponded to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N (Fig. 2C) [34]. In Fig. 2D, the B 1s spectrum contained three main peaks at 190.7, 192.2 and 193.1 eV, which ascribed to the BC2O, BCO2 and BO, respectively [22].

As presented in Fig. 3A, the MnO2 nanosheets displayed a characteristic 2D and sheet-like morphology. As shown in Fig. 3B, the FT-IR spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets exhibited several peaks located at 3405, 1643 and 521 cm−1, which related to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO and MnO, severally [30,35]. The XPS spectrum depicted the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Mn 2p peaks at 284.8, 400.0, 531.5, 641.8 and 653.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 3C). In Fig. 3D, the Mn 2p peaks centered at 641.9 eV and 653.4 eV were assigned to the Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, which confirmed the existence of Mn (IV) [36].

In Fig. 4A, it was observed that the fluorescence peaks of B, N-CDs was shifted with the excitation wavelength increasing from 290 nm to 380 nm, which meant the B, N-CDs presented an excitation-dependent performance. And this might be a result of non-uniform particle size distribution and different surface defect states of B, N-CDs [31]. The fluorescence spectra showed the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the B, N-CDs at 320 and 400 nm, respectively. The quantum yield of the B, N-CDs was calculated to be about 18% according to the above mentioned equation. As shown in Fig. 4B, there was a broad absorption peak at around 270 nm, which may be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bond [22].

In Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs remained nearly unchanged with variation of ionic strength (NaCl, 0–1.0 mol·L−1). As shown in Fig. 5B, it was noted that the fluorescence intensity was slightly higher when pH value was 7.0, and the emission of B, N-CDs generally kept stable in a wide pH range from 3.0 to 12.0. On the whole, the fluorescence performance of B, N-CDs was stable, demonstrating good photostability and great practical application effect.

For the practical utility, the influence of pH, reaction time and the volume of MnO2 nanosheets on the system were explored. As shown in Fig. 6A and B, the FL intensity reached the maximum value when the pH value was 7.0 in the B,N-CDs/MnO2/GSH and B,N-CDs/MnO2/GSH/Ag+ system, respectively. In Fig. 6C and D, it was observed that the FL intensity showed a nearly constant when the reaction time was 20 min. In Fig. 6E, F and F
0 represented the FL intensity in the presence and in the absence of GSH, respectively. It can be seen that the maximum ∆F (F - F
0 ) was obtained when the volume of MnO2 nanosheets was 400 μL. Therefore, pH 7.0, 20 min of reaction time and 400 μL of MnO2 nanosheets were selected as optimal reaction conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of GSH and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
In Fig. 7A, the fluorescence spectra of the B, N-CDs/MnO2/GSH system in the presence of varied concentrations of GSH were investigated. The fluorescence signal of the system was restored with the concentration of GSH increasing. The value of F − F
0 linearly increased with changing of GSH concentration in the area of 1.0 to 90.0 μmol·L−1 with the correlation coefficients (R 2) was 0.9957, and the linear relationship was concluded as F − F
0 = 93.62c GSH − 28.57, where F and F
0 refer to the FL intensity with GSH and without GSH, separately (Fig. 7B). The detection limit of GSH detection was 0.32 μmol·L−1 according to 3σ/k rule (σ refers to standard deviation and k means the slope of the calibration curve).

As shown in Fig. 7C, the FL intensity of the B, N-CDs/MnO2/GSH/Ag+ system was gradually decreased as the concentration of Ag+ varied from 5.0 to 70.0 μmol·L−1. In Fig. 7D, there was a good linear relationship between F
0 – F and Ag+ concentration with the linear range was 0.81 to 70.0 μmol·L−1, which was described by the equation of F
0 – F = 92.82c Ag+ + 367.45 (R 2 = 0.9974) (where F and F
0 refer to the FL intensity with Ag+ and without Ag+, separately). And the detection limit was calculated to be 0.24 μmol·L−1 based on 3σ/k rule.

Moreover, the detection performance of the reported probes and this probe were illustrated in Table 1 . Compared with the reported probes, the proposed probe displayed a lower or comparable linear range and detection limit. Therefore, these results demonstrated that the B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system had a great potential to be developed as a fluorescence probe for GSH and Ag+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of GSH and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8A showed the fluorescence spectra of the B, N-CDs in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets, GSH and Ag+, respectively. It was observed that GSH, Ag+, GSH + Ag+ nearly had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the B, N-CDs, while the addition of MnO2 nanosheets quenched the fluorescence of the B, N-CDs. And it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system was recovered with the addition of GSH. As a kind of antioxidant, GSH could lead to the decomposition of the MnO2 nanosheets and reduce the MnO2 to Mn2+, preventing the quenching effect on the B, N-CDs (Eq. (1)).
(1)
MnO 2
+ 2GSH +
2H +
→
Mn
2 +
+ GSSG +
2H 2
O

However, when Ag+ was introduced into the B, N-CDs/MnO2/GSH system, the binding interaction between Ag+ and GSH would inhibit the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets, resulting in the decrease of the fluorescence intensity of the system. In this progress, the fluorescence of the proposed probe switched from “off” to “on” and “off” again.

Fig. 8B showed the fluorescence spectra of B, N-CDs and UV–vis spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets. There was a broad UV–vis absorption peak from 200 nm to 600 nm, which was a large overlap between the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of B, N-CDs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 8C, there was no evident change between the fluorescence lifetimes of the B, N-CDs before (4.04 ns) and after adding MnO2 nanosheets (3.79 ns). In Fig. 8D, the UV–vis absorption peak of B, N-CDs showed no obvious change after the addition of MnO2 nanosheets, demonstrating that there was no complex formed. In summary, the most possible sensing mechanism between B, N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets might be IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for determination of GSH and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, the selectivity of the proposed fluorescence probe was also explored. As shown in Fig. 9A, a number of interference substances including ascorbic acid (AA, 80 μmol·L−1), cysteine (Cys, 80 μmol·L−1), glucose (800 μmol·L−1) and amino acids (800 μmol·L−1) such as alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), serine (Ser), proline (Pro), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu) and some common ions (800 μmol·L−1) such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Mn2+ were analyzed using the B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system. Although ascorbic acid and cysteine both had effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system, their concentrations was much lower compared with the concentration of GSH in biological systems [7,46]. Therefore, there was a negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of the system with the addition of interferents, indicating the highly selectivity toward GSH detection. For Ag+ detection, various concentrations of inorganic ionic such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ were added into the B, N-CDs/MnO2/GSH system (Fig. 9B). It can be seen that Cu2+ could cause significant change on the fluorescence intensity of this system. However, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) was a kind of typical masking agent which could chelate with Cu2+ and produced more stable complexes [47]. Therefore, the B, N-CDs/MnO2/GSH system could be served as a selective probe for Ag+ detection. And all these results indicated that the B, N -CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system was a potentially high selective probe for GSH and Ag+ determination.

In this work, the human serum samples and tap water samples were assayed to estimate the practicability of the fluorescence probe for the detection of GSH and Ag+ (Table 2 ). Standard addition method was used to detect the concentrations of GSH and Ag+ in these samples, and all results were taken from five repeated experiments. For GSH determination, the human serum samples were diluted to 100 times and spiked in different concentrations of GSH and detected with the proposed assay. The recovery was calculated to be 98.10% to 102.20% and relative standard deviation (RSD) ranges from 2.67% to 3.81%. And for Ag+ determination, these samples were measured by adding standard solution of Ag+ into tap water samples. Satisfying recovery was obtained, which was 98.70% to 102.25% with RSD within a range of 1.65% to 4.01%. These results suggested that the B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system was reliable for the practical applications.

In this work, B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets based “off-on-off” fluorescent probe for GSH and Ag+ detection was constructed. In the B, N-CDs/MnO2 nanosheets system, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by MnO2 nanosheets through IFE. After the introduction of GSH, MnO2 nanosheets were decomposed by the reductive GSH, leading to the fluorescence intensity enhancement. While upon the addition of Ag+, the highly combining specificity between GSH and Ag+ prevented the destruction of MnO2 nanosheets, which decreased the fluorescence of the system and thereby fluorometric quantification assay of Ag+ was obtained. Moreover, this nanoprobe showed good performance with high sensitivity and selectivity, which facilitated quantitative determination of GSH and Ag+ in human serum samples and water samples. In brief, these findings demonstrated it was a simple and efficient method for GSH and Ag+ detection toward practical application.
Jun Zhang: Conducting experiments, writing original draft preparation.
Huan Yang: Data curation.
Shuang Pan: Investigation.
Hui Liu: Supervision.
Xiaoli Hu: Reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705132) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2019TY003).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. E. Zhao, C. Lei, Y. Gao, H. Gao, S. Zhu, X. Yang, J. You, H. Wang, A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 253 (2017) 239-246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A reusable bifunctional fluorescent sensor for the detection and removal of silver ions in aqueous solutions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wu, J. jJia, Y. Xu, X. Qian, W. Zhu, A reusable bifunctional fluorescent sensor for the detection and removal of silver ions in aqueous solutions, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 265 (2018) 59-66.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel method for the detection of silver ions with carbon dots: excellent selectivity, fast response, low detection limit and good applicability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. C. Jin, B. B. Wang, Z. Q. Xu, X. H. He, H. F. Zou, Q. Q. Yang, F. L. Jiang, Y. Liu, A novel method for the detection of silver ions with carbon dots: excellent selectivity, fast response, low detection limit and good applicability, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 267 (2018) 627-635.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of silver ions by using sulfur quantum dots modified gold electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Fu, A. Wang, K. Xie, J. Zhu, F. Chen, H. Wang, H. Zhang, W. Su, Z. Wang, C. Zhou, Electrochemical detection of silver ions by using sulfur quantum dots modified gold electrode, Sens. Actuators B: chem. 304 (2020) 127390.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticles and cleavage-based dual signal amplification for ultrasensitive detection of silver ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Miao, L. Ning, X. Li, Gold nanoparticles and cleavage-based dual signal amplification for ultrasensitive detection of silver ions, Anal. Chem. 85 (2013) 7966-7970.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activatable core–shell metallofullerene: an efficient nanoplatform for bimodal sensing of glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Peng, C. Ye, R. Yan, Y. Lei, D. Ye, H. Hong, T. Cai, Activatable core–shell metallofullerene: an efficient nanoplatform for bimodal sensing of glutathione, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 11 (2019) 46637-46644.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive glutathione detection based on lucigenin cathodic electrochemiluminescence in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Gao, Z. Liu, L. Qi, J. Lai, S.A. Kitte, G. Xu, Ultrasensitive glutathione detection based on lucigenin cathodic electrochemiluminescence in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 7654-7659.
